dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a reported hemorrhage or lacerations at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm R. Austin, a paramedic with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been experiencing nausea and vomiting for three days. I saw blood in my vomit, so I called.
I don't see signs of overt bleeding or lacerations. When did the blood first appear, and what were you doing then?
No chest or breathing symptoms. The blood came up when I was vomiting harder-maybe due to straining.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Your GCS score is 15.
Your blood pressure is currently 134 over 87.
Pulse is 90 beats per minute.
Respirations are 17 breaths per minute and regular.
Oxygen saturation is 100%.
Blood glucose is 103. That's in the normal range.
We're placing you on a cardiac monitor now with 4-lead ECG for monitoring.
You mentioned not being able to keep anything down. Can you talk more about when the problem started?
I've been vomiting since before week 13 of my pregnancy. I haven't had an appointment yet.
Have you had a prior OB-GYN visit or have any known allergies or conditions?
No prior issues or allergies.
Your skin is pink, warm, and dry. I can feel strong radial pulses.
Your airway is clear and patent.
Do you currently feel any pain or discomfort other than the weakness?
I don't feel pain ongoing. Just some discomfort from the nausea.
Given the dehydration signs, we're starting a 700 mL IV fluid bolus in your left arm.
We're also giving you 4 mg of Zofran IV to help with nausea.
Your blood pressure has dropped to 107 over 65, and your pulse is 68 now.
Blood glucose is currently undetectable.
Your breathing remains at 17 per minute and oxygen is 100%.
On the later check, blood pressure is 115 over 67.
Did you notice any improvement after these treatments?
Yes, I feel a bit better now, more comfortable.
You are 13 weeks pregnant, so we will transport you to the L&D area at VCU for specialized care.
Since your condition is stable, we're doing a cold transport with continuous monitoring.
You're secured now on the stretcher for transport. Everything is in place.
We're transporting you to VCU Health for observation and further care.
VCU ER knows to expect you, and Labor & Delivery is prepped for your arrival.
We will give a phone report en route to allow their team to start preparing your care.
Before we head out, may I get your consent for treatment, transport, and billing?
Yes, I give my full consent now.
We'll move you carefully with the stretcher to the ambulance for transport.
Upon arrival, we'll give an in-person report to the RN in the Labor & Delivery unit.
You will be taken to room A in Labor & Delivery where the ER team will take over care.
You report no ongoing pain, with a pain score of 0.
This case is handled under Protocol 3-9 for medical management of nausea and vomiting.
